title: podman attach: There Can Be Only One (tty)
body:
Synopsis: podman run, then podman attach, the latter steals stdout (but not stdin) from the former:
```
w1# podman run --name foo --interactive fedora cat
hi from run     <-- type this
hi from run     <-- get this
```
Now, in a separate window:
```
w2# podman attach foo
hi from attach    <-- type this
hi from attach    <-- get this
```
Note that `w1` does not get `hi from attach`. Should it? With docker, output goes to both.

Now go back to `w1` and type `hi again from run`. It will not echo in `w1`, but will echo (in real time) in `w2`. Hit Enter, and the second copy (from `cat`) is also output in `w2` but not `w1`. `^D` in either window terminates the container.

podman-0.3.5-1.gitdb6bf9e.fc27.x86_64

comments.body:
Given that all our terminal ops are happening over a unix socket, I don't think there is any inherent reason why we can't have multiple connections going at once.

As it sounds now, though, I think conmon is probably dumping the first connection after another is made

Yeah, confirmed, `conmon` stores only one connection socket - https://github.com/kubernetes-incubator/cri-o/blob/master/conmon/conmon.c#L587

Once a new connection comes in, that FD is overwritten, and `conmon` stops copying to it.

We should theoretically be able to make that a list of FDs and copy to all of them instead of just one. I'll open an issue upstream.
Makes sense to me.
CRI-O https://github.com/kubernetes-incubator/cri-o/pull/1483 should fix
CRI-O PR was merged. We should pix up the fix in the next release.
Fixed in podman-0.6.2-10.git72c0b4f.fc28.x86_64

Using  podman-0.6.2-10.git72c0b4f.fc28.x86_64 in one vm, I am not getting the echo in that example command.

```
# podman run --name foo --interactive fedora:28 cat
Hello
^C^\^Z^Z
```
Only pressing `Ctrl+d` disconnects from the container.
@kushaldas Multiple attaches (a `podman attach` after `podman run`) or just one?

@kushaldas first of all, I would not expect ^C or ^Z to have any effect on an interactive cat. However: in your excerpt, are you typing `Hello` followed by enter, and then _not seeing a second Hello_? That's alarming. If this is the case, what is your `$TERM`?

>@kushaldas Multiple attaches (a podman attach after podman run) or just one?

In both cases.

>what is your `$TERM`?

```
$ echo $TERM
xterm-256color
```

Hm. Sending EOF via `Ctrl-D` exits attach... If you run a `bash` container and type in `exit`, will it also exit? I want to see if input is being correctly forwarded into the container. Output is definitely not making it out.
@mheon if I use `-it`, then nothing happens on the terminal, it just stays hanged there.
Alright, so input is not being forwarded either, then

>Hm. Sending EOF via Ctrl-D exits attach... If you run a bash container and type in exit, will it also exit? I want to see if input is being correctly forwarded into the container. Output is definitely not making it out.

It seems so, 

```
# podman run --name foo2 --interactive fedora:28 bash
exit
#
```
Huh. So it is forwarding input without `-t` then. That's strange.

Got a shell on the system where this reproduces. I think I've tracked down the cause - syslog is getting spammed with 
`conmon[5988]: conmon 7fb208f490dfd2229a50 <nwarn>: write_k8s_log failed`

Not sure why yet, checking the `conmon` code to see if anything jumps out
The error seems to be in `conmon` in `set_k8s_timestamp()` - https://github.com/kubernetes-incubator/cri-o/blob/master/conmon/conmon.c#L284-L316
I initially thought this was going to be an issue accessing the realtime clock or something similar, but we are actually producing timestamps too long for the conmon buffer

For reference, the generated timestamp:
`2018-06-21T01:33:55.102183860+05:1800 stdout `
This comes out to 45 bytes long, while `conmon` wants only under 44 bytes for a timestamp
It's the `off % 3600`. That should be '(off % 3600) / 60.
